coredata.dc:title: Graphene oxide quantum dots (GOQDs) induce behavioral disorders via the disturbance of kynurenine pathway in zebrafish larvae
coredata.prism:publicationName: Comparative Biochemistry and Physiology Part C: Toxicology & Pharmacology
coredata.dc:creator: Deng, Shun
Zhang, Enming
Zhao, Yunyang
Guo, Hao
Luo, Lan
Yi, Song
Zheng, Bingxin
Mu, Xiaoyuan
Yuan, Kezhu
Deng, Xuangen
Rong, Haibo
Ma, Yanbo
Bian, Wanping
Shen, Hai
coredata.dc:description: The emergence of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) expands the use of graphene derivatives in nanomedicine for its direct therapeutic applications in treating neurodegeneration, inflammation, metabolic dysfunction, and among others. Nevertheless, the biosafety assessment of GQDs remains deficient mostly because of the diverse surface characteristics of the nanoparticles. Our prior work demonstrated that GQDs can induce strong thigmotactic effects in zebrafish larvae over a wide range of concentrations, yet the underlying metabolic mechanisms remain largely unknown. In this study, we conducted a further exploration about graphene oxide quantum dots (GOQDs) for its potential neurotoxic effect on the behaviors of zebrafish larvae by combining neurotransmitter-targeted metabolomics with locomotion analysis. After continuous exposure to a concentration gradient of GOQDs (12.5 - 25 – 50 – 100 - 200 μg/mL) for 7 days, the thigmotactic activities of zebrafish larvae were observed across all exposure concentrations relative to the control group, while the basal locomotor activities, including distance moved and average velocity, were significantly changed by low concentrations of GOQDs. Targeted metabolomics was performed using zebrafish larvae at 7 days post-fertilization (dpf) that were exposed to 12.5 and 200 μg/mL, both of which were found to perturb the kynurenine pathway by regulating the levels of kynurenine, 3-hydroxyanthranilic acid (3-HAA), and quinolinic acid (QA). Furthermore, the thigmotaxis of larval fish induced by GOQDs during exposure could be counteracted by supplementing Ro-61-8048, an agonist acting on kynurenine 3-monooxygenase (KMO). In conclusion, our study establishes the involvement of the kynurenine pathway in GOQDs-induced thigmotaxis, which is independent of the transcriptional modulation of glutamate receptor families.
coredata.dcterms:subject: GOQDs
Thigmotaxis
Zebrafish
Metabolomics
Kynurenine pathway

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Rescue of the neurobehavioral change induced by GQDs
Low concentrations of GOQDs induce thigmotactic response
Thigmotaxis is induced by GOQDs via the perturbation of KYN pathway and independent of glutamate receptors

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Central Government Funds of Guiding Local Scientific and Technological Development for Sichuan Province

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the research project from Central Government Funds of Guiding Local Scientific and Technological Development for Sichuan Province ( 2021ZYD0068 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Surface characteristics of GOQDs. A, TEM image of GOQDs and the insert indicates the distribution of nanoscales, which were measured and normalized from 30 nanoparticles using NanoScope Analysis 1.5 software (Bruker). B, FTIR spectra of GOQDs.
A simple graphic depiction of the experimental design.

Statistical data of the locomotor activities of zebrafish larvae exposed to GOQDs. A, the total distance moved of zebrafish larvae exposed to a concentration gradient of GOQDs (12.5 – 25 - 50 – 100 – 200 μg/mL), and each treatment was performed with 24 larval fish. B, the segmental analysis of the average distance moved of larvae per 2 min across the light-dark period. C, the cumulative duration (CD) of larvae wandering in the border or outer regions in different treatment. D, heatmap showing of the changes of CD per 2 min during the whole tracking period. Data between control and GOQDs treatments were compared by means of One-way ANOVA and the values of ⁎ p ≤ 0.05, ⁎⁎ p ≤ 0.01, and ⁎⁎⁎ p ≤ 0.001 are considered as significant levels.

Neurotransmitter-targeted metabolic analysis. A, principal component analysis (PCA) of the multivariant components from different treatment, each treatment was performed with five replicates (n = 500 embryos per replicate). B, a list of the significantly changed neurotransmitters (ng/mL) between control and GOQDs treatments was analyzed using One-Way ANOVA and the significant levels were presented as the following: ⁎ p ≤ 0.05, ⁎⁎ p ≤ 0.01, and ⁎⁎⁎ p ≤ 0.001.

Rescue treatment of zebrafish larvae exposed to 12.5 μg/mL GOQDs with 1 mM Ro-61-8048. A, an exhibition of the motion trails of zebrafish larvae using the automatic tracking recording collected from different time intervals. B, the total distance moved of zebrafish larvae in different treatment, each treatment was performed using 24 larval zebrafish. C, the cumulative duration (CD) of larvae in different treatment. D–E, the levels of quinolinic acid (QA) and kynurenic acid (KYA) quantified in different treatment. Independent sample t-test was used to determine the significant differences between control and other treatments. Values of ⁎ p < 0.05, ⁎⁎ p < 0.01, and ⁎⁎⁎ p < 0.001 are considered to be significant levels.

qPCR analysis for glutamate receptors family members. One-Way ANOVA test was employed to compare the relative mRNA expression levels between control and other treatments. Each treatment was carried out with four replicates (n = 50 embryos per replicate), and the significant levels were described as ⁎ p ≤ 0.05, ⁎⁎ p ≤ 0.01, and ⁎⁎⁎ p ≤ 0.001.

head.ce:title: Graphene oxide quantum dots (GOQDs) induce behavioral disorders via the disturbance of kynurenine pathway in zebrafish larvae

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Chongqing Institute of Green and Intelligent Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Chongqing institute of green and intelligent technology, Chinese Academy of Science, Chongqing 400714, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The emergence of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) expands the use of graphene derivatives in nanomedicine for its direct therapeutic applications in treating neurodegeneration, inflammation, metabolic dysfunction, and among others. Nevertheless, the biosafety assessment of GQDs remains deficient mostly because of the diverse surface characteristics of the nanoparticles. Our prior work demonstrated that GQDs can induce strong thigmotactic effects in zebrafish larvae over a wide range of concentrations, yet the underlying metabolic mechanisms remain largely unknown. In this study, we conducted a further exploration about graphene oxide quantum dots (GOQDs) for its potential neurotoxic effect on the behaviors of zebrafish larvae by combining neurotransmitter-targeted metabolomics with locomotion analysis. After continuous exposure to a concentration gradient of GOQDs (12.5 - 25 – 50 – 100 - 200 μg/mL) for 7 days, the thigmotactic activities of zebrafish larvae were observed across all exposure concentrations relative to the control group, while the basal locomotor activities, including distance moved and average velocity, were significantly changed by low concentrations of GOQDs. Targeted metabolomics was performed using zebrafish larvae at 7 days post-fertilization (dpf) that were exposed to 12.5 and 200 μg/mL, both of which were found to perturb the kynurenine pathway by regulating the levels of kynurenine, 3-hydroxyanthranilic acid (3-HAA), and quinolinic acid (QA). Furthermore, the thigmotaxis of larval fish induced by GOQDs during exposure could be counteracted by supplementing Ro-61-8048, an agonist acting on kynurenine 3-monooxygenase (KMO). In conclusion, our study establishes the involvement of the kynurenine pathway in GOQDs-induced thigmotaxis, which is independent of the transcriptional modulation of glutamate receptor families.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are ultrasmall nanoparticles (NPs) derived from graphene with lateral dimensions typically below 10 nm, and the NPs exhibit excellent photoelectric properties resulting from quantum confinement and edge effects (Ghaffarkhah et al., 2022). GQDs hold great promise in biomedicine due to its large surface areas, good photostability, easily-manipulated wavelength bands, high thermal conductivity, and favorable biocompatibility, whereby the NPs have been engineered for drug carriers, fluorophores, biosensors, photodynamic/photothermal agents, etc. (Biswas et al., 2021; Sengupta et al., 2023). Impressively, an increasing number of studies during the past few years showed that GQDs can be independently employed as small molecular drugs to treat multiple diseases, such as Parkinson's disease, colitis, retinopathy, and Niemann-Pick disease type C (NPC) (Kang et al., 2021; Kim et al., 2018; Lee et al., 2020; Zhao et al., 2022). However, the potential toxicity of GQDs remains to be investigated for the difference of carbon sources and fabrication methods, the diverse surface characterization, atom doping, etc.

There has been clear evidence that at least three types of GQDs can induce apoptosis at relatively low concentrations. It was firstly reported that nitrogen-doped GQDs (N-GQDs) at 50 μg/mL could cause significant mortality of zebrafish embryos after 6 days of successive exposure, along with the remarkable decrease of heartbeat rate and locomotion activity (Wang et al., 2015). The toxicity mechanisms of N-GQDs were elucidated in a few follow-up studies, which showed that the NPs could initiate apoptotic signals via disrupting a hierarchical ensemble of pathways involving MAPK, cytochrome P450 system, lysosome, ferroptosis, etc., as well as the balance of redox system (Deng et al., 2019; Deng et al., 2018; Wu et al., 2022; Zhang et al., 2017). Hydroxylated GQDs (HO-GQDs) are now recognized as high toxic nanoparticles that at a low exposure concentration (25 μg/mL) exerted deleterious effects on DNA, cell cycle, microtube structure in human esophageal epithelial cells (HET-1A) (Li et al., 2018). In vivo toxicity assays further confirmed that HO-GQDs brough about serious intestinal injuries, which were characteristic of enhanced intestinal penetrability, shortened villi, and crypt loss (Yu et al., 2019). Aminated GQDs (A-GQDs) share similarities with N-GQDs in the element composition and toxicity mechanisms (Wu et al., 2023; Zhang et al., 2017), and was also reported for its ability to break down the DNA strands via H-bonding and π-π stacking (Xu et al., 2018). Except the above three toxic GQDs, a latest in vivo experiment revealed that four types of GQDs including reduced GQDs (R-GQDs), graphene oxide QDs (GOQDs), carboxylated GQDs (C-GQDs), and A-GQDs could trigger the similar transcriptomic responses associated with calcium/potassium ion channels and spliceosomes (Deng et al., 2022). A subsequent study manifested the excellent biocompatibility of R-GQDs and GQODs, both of which did not arouse significant mortality of zebrafish embryos at the highest concentrations (0.96 mg/mL and 2.4 mg/mL, respectively). Also, GOQDs have been reported for its medicinal potential in the treatment of neurotoxicity, alcoholism, amyloidosis, etc. (Ren et al., 2018; Rostampour Ghareghozloo et al., 2020; Sun et al., 2017). Interestingly, it was observed that zebrafish larvae exhibited strong thigmotactic response when exposed to a broad spectrum of concentrations of R-GQDs (150, 300, 600, 1200, and 2400 μg/mL) and GOQDs (60, 120, 240, 480, and 960 μg/mL), and the thigmotaxis was caused possibly by the alteration of the total glutamine concentration and the transcriptomic perturbation of the glutamate receptor families (Deng et al., 2023). However, it remains whether the thigmotactic effect could be induced in zebrafish larvae exposed to much lower concentrations when compared to that used in the previous study, and if so, whether the dystropic behavior is governed by the abovementioned factors or others? Thus, more in-depth studies would be required to find out the underlying mechanisms of thigmotactic effect aroused by GQDs.

Thigmotaxis is a validated index of anxiety, and the larval zebrafish have been proven for its effectiveness in behavior assessment that is comparable to that observed in adult fish (Collier et al., 2017; Mrinalini et al., 2023). In the present study, we employed targeted metabolomics to investigate the changes of 47 neurotransmitters in zebrafish embryos treated by GOQDs. Firstly, we performed behavioral analysis using zebrafish larvae after exposure to a concentration gradient of GOQDs (12.5 - 25 – 50 – 100 - 200 μg/mL). Then, based on the behavioral data, neurotransmitter-targeted metabolomics was carried out using zebrafish larvae exposed to two concentrations of GOQDs for 7 days, respectively as 12.5 and 200 μg/mL. Next, based on the metabolomic data mining, we investigated whether the thigmotactic effect was changed in zebrafish larvae under the simultaneous exposure of GOQDs and the selective compound targeting specific neurometabolic pathway. We also performed qPCR to examine the transcriptional changes of several genes from the glutamine receptor family. Our study revealed the neurotoxic effect of GQDs on animals via the disturbance of kynurenine way, thereby providing an alert for the medicinal uses of GQDs to treat CNS-related diseases.

Animal experiments were carried out by complying with the ‘Guide for the Care and Use of Laboratory Animals’ (Eighth Edition, 2011. ILARCLS, National Research Council, Washington, D.C.). The animal protocol was approved by the Animal Care and Use Committee of Chongqing in China and by the Institutional Animal Care and Use Committee of Chongqing Institute of Green and Intelligent Technology, Chinese Academy of Science (Approval ID: ZKCQY0367).

Graphene oxide quantum dots (GOQDs, XF074) was purchased from Nanjing XFNANO Materials Tech Co., Ltd (XFNANO, Nanjing, China), and GOQDs were fabricated from the source of graphene using the top-down method. The size distribution of GOQDs was investigated using a Tecnai G2 20 Transmission Electron Microscope (TEM) (FEI Co.), and the basic functional groups of GOQDs were determined with an Agilent Cary 630 FTIR Spectrometer (Agilent Technologies Inc., Santa Clara, CA, USA).

Ro-61-8048 (powder, 98 % purity) and 3-Aminobenzoic acid ethyl ester methanesulfonate (MS-222, powder, 98 % purity) were both purchased from Aladdin Chemistry Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Ro-61-8048 was dissolved with DMSO into a stock solution of 100 mM and MS-222 was dissolved with ultrapure water (ddH2O) into a stock solution of 20 mg/mL. The ELISA kits to quantify the levels of quinolinic acid (QA) and kynurenic acid (KYA) of zebrafish larvae were ordered from Cloud-Clone Corporation (Wuhan, China), respectively as CEK552Ge and CED718Ge (Product No.).

About 500 zebrafish (Danio rerio) at 4 months old were ordered from China Zebrafish Resource Center (CZRC, Wuhan, China) and maintained at the condition described in the previous study (Deng et al., 2022). After 7 days' acclimatization, the adult zebrafish were paired for copulation and the laid eggs were collected at 2 h post fertilization (hpf). For the behavioral assessment and following assays, zebrafish embryos at 4 hpf were employed for exposure assays, and 50 embryos were transferred to a well of six-well plate (Corning, Shanghai, China) filled with a concentration gradient of GOQDs (12.5 – 25 -50 – 100 – 200 μg/mL), which were replaced daily with the fresh solutions, and each concentration was carried out with four replicates.

Zebrafish larvae after continuous exposed to GOQDs for 7 d were examined for the basal locomotor activity, including the total distance travelled, average velocity, acceleration, etc. Before the behavioral test, zebrafish larvae along with the exposure solution were removed from the six-well plate to a 24-well plate and acclimated for 1 h. After that, the 24-well plate was transferred to a fixed platform embedded in the DanioVision system and exposed to alternating 10-min light and dark periods for a total of 50 min. Behavioral data were automatically acquired from 24-well plates by means of EthoVision XT software (v.15.0, Noldus). With the same apparatus, we further scrutinized the larvae treated by GOQDs to ascertain their dwell time within the outer and inner regions of a circular cell, which was usually represented by cumulative duration (CD), an index set by the software. Thigmotaxis is the propensity to avoid the center of an arena and stay or move in close proximity to the boundaries of the environment (Schnörr et al., 2012), and thus the CD between the outer and inner regions serves as an indicator for the likelihood of thigmotactic effects occurring in larval fish.

The embryos for the metabolomic analysis were treated in a ground glass tank (20 × 15 cm), and 500 embryos per replicate for different treatment were prepared, including control, 12.5 and 200 μg/mL GOQDs. Each treatment was performed with five replicates, and a total of 2500 embryos were collected. After exposure, zebrafish larvae at 7 dpf were firstly anesthetized by MS-222 (20 mg/L) and then treated by liquid nitrogen and placed in a −80 °C refrigerator. After the sample was thawed and crushed, an amount of 50 mg of the sample was treated with 500 μL of 70 % methanol/water. The mixture was vortexed for 3 min under the condition of 2500 r/min and centrifuged at 12000 r/min for 10 min at 4 °C. Take 300 μL of supernatant into a new centrifuge tube and place the supernatant in −20 °C refrigerator for 30 min. Then the supernatant was centrifuged at 12000 r/min for 10 min at 4 °C. After centrifugation, transfer 180 μL of supernatant for further LC-MS analysis.

The extract of fish sample was analyzed using an LC-ESI-MS/MS system (UPLC, ExionLC ADˈ https://sciex.com.cn/; MS, QTRAP® 6500+ System, https://sciex.com/). The analytical parameters were configured as follows for HPLC: utilizing a Waters ACQUITY UPLC HSS T3 C18 column (100 mm × 2.1 mm i.d.ˈ 1.8 μm), the solvent system comprised water with 0.1 % formic acid (A) and acetonitrile with 0.1 % formic acid (B). The gradient initiated at 5 % B (0 min), increased to 95 % B (0–8 min), remained at 95 % B (8–9.5 min), and ultimately reverted to 5 % B (9.6–12 min). The flow rate was set at 0.35 mL/min, the temperature at 40 °C, and the injection volume at 2 μL.

The AB 6500+ QTRAP® LC-MS/MS System, equipped with an ESI Turbo Ion-Spray interface, was employed in both positive and negative ion modes and controlled by Analyst 1.6 software (AB Sciex). The ESI source operation parameters were set as follows: ion source, turbo spray; source temperature 550 °C; ion spray voltage (IS) 5500 V˄ Positive˅, −4500 V(Negative); curtain gas (CUR) was set at 35.0 psi. DP (declustering potential) and CE (collision energy) for individual MRM (multiple reaction monitoring) transitions underwent further optimization. A specific set of MRM transitions were monitored for each period according to the neurotransmitters eluted within this period.

Unsupervised PCA (principal component analysis) was performed by statistics function prcomp within R (www.r-project.org). The data was unit variance scaled before unsupervised PCA. A total of 47 neurotransmitters were quantified using a calibration standard curve, and the final concentration of each neurotransmitter was determined according to the following linear equation:
ng / g = c × V / 1000 / m
c, the concentration (ng/mL) of neurotransmitters calculated by the integrated peak area ratios; V, the volume (μL) of extraction used in the study; m, the quality (g) of the sample. Significantly regulated metabolites between control and GOQDs treatments were determined by absolute Log2FC (fold change >1.5).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Rescue of the neurobehavioral change induced by GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: To further investigate the metabolic pathway underlying the thigmotactic effect induced by GOQDs, we used a kynurenine 3-monooxygenase (KMO) inhibitor Ro-61-8048 to investigate the regulatory effects of quinolinic acid (QA) on the neurobehavior of zebrafish larvae. In brief, zebrafish larvae at 6 dpf that exposed to 12.5 μg/mL of GOQDs were co-cultured with 1 mM Ro-61-8048 for 24 h, and each treatment was performed with 24 larval fish. After that, the behaviors of larvae including the total distance moved and the thigmotaxis were examined with the DanioVision system. Also, the levels of QA and KYA of zebrafish larvae in different treatment were quantified using the ELISA assay kit (CEK552Ge and CED718Ge, respectively). These two metabolites were extracted from zebrafish larvae according the following procedure. Firstly, 100 larvae per replicate were treated by MS-222 (20 mg/L) and collected in a sterile 1.5 mL centrifuge tube. After mixing with 300 μL lysis buffer, the fish were smashed with a pestle for about 1 min and the homogenate was centrifuged at 1000 ×g at 4 °C for 15 min. The supernatant was removed to a new 1.5 mL tube and prepared for ELISA detection according to the standard protocol provided by the assay kit, and each treatment was performed for four replicates.

Except for the behavioral analysis, the remaining larvae were collected from control and two GOQDs treatments (12.5 and 200 μg/mL) for qPCR assays. The total RNAs of zebrafish larvae from different treatments (12.5 and 200 μg/mL) were extracted using RNAisoPlus (Takara, Dalian, China) and the first-strand cDNAs were prepared with 1 μg of total RNA using FastKing RT kit with gDNase (TIANGEN, Beijing, China) according to the manufacturer's instruction. qPCR was performed on a Roche LightCycler 480 II (LC480, Roche Diagnostics) in a 20 μL reaction with the following running program: a pre-incubation of 95 °C for 1.5 min, and the ensuing 40 cycles: 95 °C for 5 s, 60 °C for 20 s, and 72 °C for 10s. After the end of whole cycles, a temperature of 72 °C sustained for 30s. Six genes (grin1a, gria1a, grin2a, grin3a, gria3a, and gria4a) from glutamine receptor families were examined for their transcriptional changes, and primers were designed according to the previous report (Supplementary Table 1), and ef1α was selected as the internal control reference gene for transcript normalization. 2−△△Ct method was employed for the detection of the mRNA expression levels and four replicates (50 embryos/per replicate) were carried out for each treatment.

Except for the behavioral analysis, the remaining larvae were collected from control and two GOQDs treatments (12.5 and 200 μg/mL) for qPCR assays. The total RNAs of zebrafish larvae from different treatments (12.5 and 200 μg/mL) were extracted using RNAisoPlus (Takara, Dalian, China) and the first-strand cDNAs were prepared with 1 μg of total RNA using FastKing RT kit with gDNase (TIANGEN, Beijing, China) according to the manufacturer's instruction. qPCR was performed on a Roche LightCycler 480 II (LC480, Roche Diagnostics) in a 20 μL reaction with the following running program: a pre-incubation of 95 °C for 1.5 min, and the ensuing 40 cycles: 95 °C for 5 s, 60 °C for 20 s, and 72 °C for 10s. After the end of whole cycles, a temperature of 72 °C sustained for 30s. Six genes (grin1a, gria1a, grin2a, grin3a, gria3a, and gria4a) from glutamine receptor families were examined for their transcriptional changes, and primers were designed according to the previous report (Supplementary Table 1), and ef1α was selected as the internal control reference gene for transcript normalization. 2−△△Ct method was employed for the detection of the mRNA expression levels and four replicates (50 embryos/per replicate) were carried out for each treatment.

All data were analyzed with SPSS22.0 software (IBM, USA). The comparison between control and different treatments were determined using One-way analysis of variance (ANOVA) and independent sample t-test, and the levels of p < 0.05 were deemed statistically significant.

TEM scanning revealed that the nanosizes of GOQDs were below 10 nm, varied between 4.5 nm and 9.5 nm, and an average of 7.47 nm with the typical lattice spaces (Fig. 1A). FTIR spectra demonstrated that at least five characteristic peaks occurred from 3375 cm−1 to 897 cm−1, involving stretch vibrations of -OH from 3375 to 3179 cm−1, CC at 1573 cm−1, C-OH at 1418 cm−1, symmetric stretching of OCO at 1340 cm−1, CO at 1140 cm−1, and CH at 897 cm−1 (Fig. 1B). The characteristics of GOQDs described here were basically in accordant with the previous findings (Deng et al., 2023; Deng et al., 2022).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Low concentrations of GOQDs induce thigmotactic response
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 2, the locomotion of zebrafish larvae was examined after 7 days of successive exposure. Compared to the control group, the total distance moved (TDM) increased significantly from 12.5 μg/mL to 100 μg/mL, and decreased when the concentration was above 50 μg/mL and to the extent that was comparable to that of control at the highest concentration (200 μg/mL) (Fig. 3A). Other locomotor activities including average velocity and acceleration showed the similar changes to that of TDM (Supplementary Table 2). After 10 min of dark acclimation, the locomotor activity of larvae was tracked individually for 50 min, involving five consecutive 10 min periods of alternating light and dark phases. Each phase was further split into 5 units (2 min period per unit) and a total of 25 time points were determined within the 50 min timeframe. The data collected from the distance moved per 2 min revealed that zebrafish larvae exposed to GOQDs were hyperactive during the second and third dark period, while the distance travelled during the light phase did not show a significant change in response to GOQDs, then indicating the important role of light-dark transition on the locomotion of larval fish when treated by GOQDs (Fig. 3B). Next, according to the statistical data of the cumulative duration (CD), it was found that zebrafish larvae spent much more time to wander around the border than to stay in the center of the cell, indicating that the occurrence of strong thigmotactic responses triggered by GQODs across all exposure concentrations, and it should be noted especially that the lowest concentration of GOQDs (12.5 μg/mL) aroused the strongest thigmotactic effect in larvae (Fig. 3C and Supplementary Table 3). Meanwhile, we investigated the thigmotactic behavior according to the way of time division method that was described in the above analysis of TMD, and found that larval thigmotaxis induced by GOQDs occurred in both light and dark phase at low concentrations (12.5 and 25 μg/mL) and only under the light condition when the exposure concentration was above 50 μg/mL (Fig. 3D).

As shown in Fig. 2, the locomotion of zebrafish larvae was examined after 7 days of successive exposure. Compared to the control group, the total distance moved (TDM) increased significantly from 12.5 μg/mL to 100 μg/mL, and decreased when the concentration was above 50 μg/mL and to the extent that was comparable to that of control at the highest concentration (200 μg/mL) (Fig. 3A). Other locomotor activities including average velocity and acceleration showed the similar changes to that of TDM (Supplementary Table 2). After 10 min of dark acclimation, the locomotor activity of larvae was tracked individually for 50 min, involving five consecutive 10 min periods of alternating light and dark phases. Each phase was further split into 5 units (2 min period per unit) and a total of 25 time points were determined within the 50 min timeframe. The data collected from the distance moved per 2 min revealed that zebrafish larvae exposed to GOQDs were hyperactive during the second and third dark period, while the distance travelled during the light phase did not show a significant change in response to GOQDs, then indicating the important role of light-dark transition on the locomotion of larval fish when treated by GOQDs (Fig. 3B). Next, according to the statistical data of the cumulative duration (CD), it was found that zebrafish larvae spent much more time to wander around the border than to stay in the center of the cell, indicating that the occurrence of strong thigmotactic responses triggered by GQODs across all exposure concentrations, and it should be noted especially that the lowest concentration of GOQDs (12.5 μg/mL) aroused the strongest thigmotactic effect in larvae (Fig. 3C and Supplementary Table 3). Meanwhile, we investigated the thigmotactic behavior according to the way of time division method that was described in the above analysis of TMD, and found that larval thigmotaxis induced by GOQDs occurred in both light and dark phase at low concentrations (12.5 and 25 μg/mL) and only under the light condition when the exposure concentration was above 50 μg/mL (Fig. 3D).

To understand the metabolic difference among different groups and the variance within the group, we performed principal component analysis (PCA) for 47 neurotransmitters according to the metabolic data acquired from the control and GOQDs treatments. PCA results demonstrated that PC1 score (35.51 %) of GOQDs treatments was markedly deviated from PC2 score (19.97 %) of the control group, implying the uniformity of samples from different treatments. Also, the two treatments of GOQDs (12.5 and 200 μg/mL) were basically discrete along with PC1 axis except for one sample from the treatment of 12.5 μg/mL GOQDs (Fig. 4A). Thus, the PCA data exhibited the notable changes of neurotransmitters between treatment and control groups and within the treatment groups. With the corrected p value < 0.05 and the cutoff setting (fold change (FC) > 1.5), the significantly changed neurotransmitters were screened out from two groups, respectively as control v.s 12.5 μg/mL and control v.s 200 μg/mL. The levels of three neurotransmitters including 5-methoxyindole-3-acetic acid, 3-hydroxyanthranilic acid, and kynurenine were found to be significantly regulated by both two concentrations of GOQDs (Fig. 4D and Supplementary Table 4). Specifically, the levels of dopa, 5-hydroxyindoleacetic acid, and 5-methoxytryptamine-hydrochloride were identified to be notably changed by 200 μg/mL of GOQDs, while the levels of glutathione and betaine aldehyde chloride were significantly changed by 12.5 μg/mL GOQDs (Fig. 4B).

To understand the metabolic difference among different groups and the variance within the group, we performed principal component analysis (PCA) for 47 neurotransmitters according to the metabolic data acquired from the control and GOQDs treatments. PCA results demonstrated that PC1 score (35.51 %) of GOQDs treatments was markedly deviated from PC2 score (19.97 %) of the control group, implying the uniformity of samples from different treatments. Also, the two treatments of GOQDs (12.5 and 200 μg/mL) were basically discrete along with PC1 axis except for one sample from the treatment of 12.5 μg/mL GOQDs (Fig. 4A). Thus, the PCA data exhibited the notable changes of neurotransmitters between treatment and control groups and within the treatment groups. With the corrected p value < 0.05 and the cutoff setting (fold change (FC) > 1.5), the significantly changed neurotransmitters were screened out from two groups, respectively as control v.s 12.5 μg/mL and control v.s 200 μg/mL. The levels of three neurotransmitters including 5-methoxyindole-3-acetic acid, 3-hydroxyanthranilic acid, and kynurenine were found to be significantly regulated by both two concentrations of GOQDs (Fig. 4D and Supplementary Table 4). Specifically, the levels of dopa, 5-hydroxyindoleacetic acid, and 5-methoxytryptamine-hydrochloride were identified to be notably changed by 200 μg/mL of GOQDs, while the levels of glutathione and betaine aldehyde chloride were significantly changed by 12.5 μg/mL GOQDs (Fig. 4B).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Thigmotaxis is induced by GOQDs via the perturbation of KYN pathway and independent of glutamate receptors
ce:section.ce:para: As displayed in the above metabolomic analysis, the levels of 3-hydroxyanthranilic acid (3-HAA) and kynurenine were both found to be significantly regulated by two concentrations of GOQDs, and these two metabolites belong to the canonical kynurenine (KYN) pathway and their end-product is quinolinic acid (QA), which has been reported as one neurotoxic agent. Kynurenic acid (KYA), another KYN metabolic byproduct, which is a neuroprotective agent and decreased slightly, but not to significant levels in GOQDs treated groups when compared to that in control (Supplementary Table 4). Except 3-HAA and kynurenine, both two concentrations of GOQDs were found to decrease the levels of 5-Methoxyindole-3-Acetic Acid to significant levels. To further investigate the effect of KYN pathway on the neurobehavior of zebrafish larvae, we performed a rescuing test by treating zebrafish larvae that exposed to 12.5 μg/mL GOQDs with 1 mM Ro-61-8048, an agonist acting on kynurenine 3-monooxygenase (KMO), which is responsible for converting kynurenine into 3-HAA. Results demonstrated the significantly increased TMD and strong thigmotactic response of larval fish induced by 12.5 μg/mL GOQDs that were similar to that found in the above behavioral analysis (Fig. 5A), and the solely treatment of 1 mM Ro-61-8048 did not arouse notably changes of locomotor activity in zebrafish larvae in contrast to that in control group (Fig. 5B). Noticeably, the thigmotactic effect attenuated much in larval fish under the simultaneous exposure of 12.5 μg/mL GOQDs and 1 mM Ro-61-8048 (Fig. 5C). As one of the end products of kynurenine, Elisa assay showed that the content of quinine acid (QA) exhibited the increasing trend similar to the change of 3-HAA in zebrafish larvae exposed to 12.5 μg/mL GOQDs. However, the supplement of 1 mM Ro-61-8048 decreased the level of QA to the extent that was comparable to that of control. Together, the evidence from the metabolomic, behavioral and biochemical analysis manifested the perturbative effect of GOQDs on KYN pathway via the blockade of KMO synthetic route and thus leading to the abnormal changes of 3-HAA and QA in zebrafish.

As displayed in the above metabolomic analysis, the levels of 3-hydroxyanthranilic acid (3-HAA) and kynurenine were both found to be significantly regulated by two concentrations of GOQDs, and these two metabolites belong to the canonical kynurenine (KYN) pathway and their end-product is quinolinic acid (QA), which has been reported as one neurotoxic agent. Kynurenic acid (KYA), another KYN metabolic byproduct, which is a neuroprotective agent and decreased slightly, but not to significant levels in GOQDs treated groups when compared to that in control (Supplementary Table 4). Except 3-HAA and kynurenine, both two concentrations of GOQDs were found to decrease the levels of 5-Methoxyindole-3-Acetic Acid to significant levels. To further investigate the effect of KYN pathway on the neurobehavior of zebrafish larvae, we performed a rescuing test by treating zebrafish larvae that exposed to 12.5 μg/mL GOQDs with 1 mM Ro-61-8048, an agonist acting on kynurenine 3-monooxygenase (KMO), which is responsible for converting kynurenine into 3-HAA. Results demonstrated the significantly increased TMD and strong thigmotactic response of larval fish induced by 12.5 μg/mL GOQDs that were similar to that found in the above behavioral analysis (Fig. 5A), and the solely treatment of 1 mM Ro-61-8048 did not arouse notably changes of locomotor activity in zebrafish larvae in contrast to that in control group (Fig. 5B). Noticeably, the thigmotactic effect attenuated much in larval fish under the simultaneous exposure of 12.5 μg/mL GOQDs and 1 mM Ro-61-8048 (Fig. 5C). As one of the end products of kynurenine, Elisa assay showed that the content of quinine acid (QA) exhibited the increasing trend similar to the change of 3-HAA in zebrafish larvae exposed to 12.5 μg/mL GOQDs. However, the supplement of 1 mM Ro-61-8048 decreased the level of QA to the extent that was comparable to that of control. Together, the evidence from the metabolomic, behavioral and biochemical analysis manifested the perturbative effect of GOQDs on KYN pathway via the blockade of KMO synthetic route and thus leading to the abnormal changes of 3-HAA and QA in zebrafish.

Since kynurenine and its downstream metabolites show high-affinity binding with glutamate receptor families (Tutakhail et al., 2020), we made a further PCR assay to examine the mRNA expression levels of glutamate receptor family members including α-amino-3-hydroxy-5-methyl-4-isoxazole-propionic acid (AMPA) and N-methyl-d-aspartate (NMDA) receptors, which have been reported to involve the process of behavioral disorders induced by GQDs (Deng et al., 2023). Results showed that at the lowest concentration of GOQDs (12.5 μg/mL), the expression levels of grin1a increased significantly in larvae when compared to that of control, while the other five genes including gria1a, grin2a, grin3a, gria3a, and gria4a were not significantly regulated by GOQDs from the transcriptional levels. Then together with the metabolomic analysis, the result of qPCR demonstrated that the thigmotactic behavior of zebrafish larvae was induced by KYN pathway and independent of the transcriptional changes of glutamate receptor family members (Fig. 6 ).

GOQDs are actually monolayer graphene oxide (GO) nanoparticles with ultrasmall sizes. Compared to its precursor, GOQDs are less toxic and show excellent dispersion stability in various solvents (Deng et al., 2022; Deng et al., 2023). The high biocompatibility of GOQDs has been well verified and an approximate 1 mg/mL concentration of GOQDs did not cause significant mortality of larval fish after a period of successive exposure (7 days), meanwhile, we found that the low concentration (60 μg/mL) of GOQDs did not induce any apoptosis in the brain of larvae (Deng et al., 2023). Again, the current study confirmed the hyperactivity of zebrafish larvae induced by the low concentrations of GOQDs, including the significantly increase of distance moved, average velocity, acceleration, etc. when compared to control group.

In the study, we identified the thigmotactic behaviors of zebrafish larvae when exposed to a wide range of concentrations (12.5 – 25 -50 – 100 – 200 μg/mL) of GOQDs. Together with the previous finding (Deng et al., 2023), the thigmotaxis induced by GOQDs is seemingly impervious to the exposure concentrations, implying that the thigmotaxis would be induced at much lower concentrations (<12.5 μg/mL). Through the high-throughput metabolomic analysis, we ascertained the robust perturbation exerted by GOQDs on KYN pathway across a 16-fold dose range (12.5–200 μg/mL), indicating a direct involvement of KYN pathway in the thigmotactic response prompted by GOQDs. Importantly, >95 % of tryptophan (TRP) in animals is processed via KYN pathway into two main neuroactive metabolites, respectively as kynurenic acid (KYA) and quinolinic acid (QA) (Badawy, 2017). KYA as a neuroprotective agent showed a modest decline by in larvae treated by GOQDs, while the upstream neuroactive metabolite of QA, namely 3-hydroxyanthranilic acid (3-HAA), was found to be significantly upregulated by both two concentrations of GOQDs, and 3-HAA has been also proven as a neurotoxic agent that could inhibit mitochondrion respiration and promote oxidative damage on proteins (Sas et al., 2007). The level of QA is too low to be detected under normal physiological state, and the increased levels of QA usually correlate with the certain neurodegenerative diseases (Guillemin, 2012). In the rescue treatment, the level of QA was determined by means of ELISA assay, which revealed the change of QA similar to that of 3-HAA in zebrafish larvae treated by 12.5 μg/mL when compared to that of control. R0-61-8048, a well-known kynurenine monooxygenase (KMO) inhibitor (Dounay et al., 2015), could greatly enhance the level of KYA in zebrafish larvae and reverse the decline trend of KYA resulted from the treatment of GOQDs. Since both 3-HAA and QA are neurotoxins that can induce the production of reactive oxygen species (ROS), it meant that GOQDs could cause oxidative damage to the neuronal regions associated with the production of 3-HAA and QA. However, the metabolomic data showed the elevated level of glutathione in larval fish exposed to 12.5 μg/mL GOQDs when compared to that in control and the treatment of 200 μg/mL GOQDs. Glutathione as a systemic protectant is capable of scavenging the free radicals caused by xenobiotics, thus implying that low dose of GOQDs could activate in vivo antioxidant system to protect the cellular components from oxidative stress. The effects of GOQDs on the redox system were also reported in previous studies, some of which proven the peroxidase-like activity of GOQDs (Huang et al., 2022; Ren et al., 2018; Sun et al., 2017), and the others reported the oxidative function of GOQDs (Li et al., 2020; Yan et al., 2022; Zhang et al., 2018). Thus, whether the aberrant change of 3-HAA and QA caused by GOQDs through the way of redox imbalance or other mechanisms remains to be investigated in the further.

Next, QA acts as a glutamate agonist binding to and potentiating Mg2+-sensitive N-methyl-d-aspartate (NMDA) receptors that could lead to excitotoxicity (Lugo-Huitron et al., 2013). In the previous studies, we found that the mRNA expression levels of some glutamate receptor family members were congruently downregulated by different types of GQDs to varying extent upon the exposure concentrations (Deng et al., 2022), thus, whether the thigmotactic response of zebrafish larvae induced by GOQDs at low concentrations is paralleled by the transcriptional changes of the glutamate receptor family members? qPCR assay revealed that the lowest concentration of GOQDs (12.5 μg/mL) did not cause significant changes of the mRNA levels of NMDA and AMPA receptors. Therefore, our data clearly demonstrated that the thigmotactic behavior aroused by GOQDs is solely induced by KYN pathway and independent of the transcriptional regulation of glutamate receptor families. Except GOQDs, there has been an earlier study reporting the stimulatory effect of silver nanoparticles on the levels of kynurenine in the rat brain (Adeyemi et al., 2019).

Although KYN pathway was manifested as the inducible factor underlying the thigmotaxis caused by GOQDs, the present study did not point out which way GOQDs affect the production of kynurenine, 3-HAA, QA, and among others. One of our previous studies showed that 100 μg/mL GOQDs did not induce significantly changes of mRNA expression levels of key enzymes from KYN pathway, including indoleamine dioxygenase (ido), kynurenine monooxygenase (kmo), kynurenine aminotransferase (kat), etc. (Deng et al., 2022). Thus, whether GOQDs directly interact with these genes via the protein levels or other post-translational modification remains to be investigated in further studies.

The following are the supplementary data related to this article.
Supplementary Table 1
Primers pairs for qPCR.
Supplementary Table 1
Supplementary Table 2
Comparative analysis of the basal locomoter activity of zebrafish larvae under different treatment.
Supplementary Table 2
Supplementary Table 3
Comparative analysis of the thigmotactic response of zebrafish larvae under different treatment.
Supplementary Table 3
Supplementary Table 4
Comparison of the neurotransmitters between control and different GOQDs treatment.
Supplementary Table 4
Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cbpc.2024.109865.

Shun Deng: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Data curation, Conceptualization. Enming Zhang: Writing – original draft, Formal analysis, Data curation. Yunyang Zhao: Validation, Software, Methodology. Hao Guo: Software, Resources, Methodology, Investigation. Lan Luo: Resources, Methodology. Song Yi: Investigation, Data curation. Bingxin Zheng: Resources. Xiaoyuan Mu: Resources, Project administration. Kezhu Yuan: Validation, Software, Resources. Xuangen Deng: Supervision, Resources, Project administration. Haibo Rong: Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation. Yanbo Ma: Resources. Wanping Bian: Resources. Hai Shen: Supervision, Resources, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the research project from Central Government Funds of Guiding Local Scientific and Technological Development for Sichuan Province (2021ZYD0068).

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Zhao et al., 2022

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Graphene quantum dots rescue angiogenic retinopathy via blocking STAT3/Periostin/ERK signaling

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Zhao, N., Gui, X., Fang, Q., Zhang, R., Zhu, W., Zhang, H., Li, Q., Zhou, Y., Zhao, J., Cui, X., Gao, G., Tang, H., Shen, N., Chen, T., Song, H., Shen, W., 2022. Graphene quantum dots rescue angiogenic retinopathy via blocking STAT3/Periostin/ERK signaling. J Nanobiotechnology 20, 174.
